s3.q: A normal adequate uterine contraction has the following characteristics:
options: Frequency 1 in 5 min, Duration 30 sec, Amplitude 40 mmHg
Frequency 1 in 3 min, Duration 45 sec, Amplitude 60 mmHg
Frequency 2 in 10 min, Duration 60 sec, Amplitude 80 mmHg
Frequency 1 in 2 min, Duration 20 sec, Amplitude 30 mmHg
s3.explain: The '3·45·60' rule: Frequency 1 in 3 min, Duration 45 sec, Amplitude 60 mmHg, with basal tone 5-20 mmHg.

s3.q: The normal basal tone of uterine muscle at relaxation during labor is:
s3.explain: Normal basal tone at relaxation is in the range of 5-20 mmHg. Elevated basal tone >20 mmHg is defined as hypertonic uterine contraction.

s3.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
s3.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.

s3.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s3.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

options: It is essential for understanding the condition
It is rarely relevant in clinical practice
It applies only to specific populations
It has been replaced by newer classifications
s3.explain: Understanding key concepts is fundamental to clinical management.

s4.q: Which method is the gold standard for measuring true intrauterine pressure in mmHg?
s4.explain: Internal tocodynamometry using an intrauterine pressure catheter (IUPC) is the gold standard, giving true pressure in mmHg. External toco gives frequency and rough duration but unreliable absolute pressure.

s6.explain: Uterine tachysystole is defined as 4 or more contractions in 10 minutes (ACOG: >5 in 10 min averaged over 30 min). Hyperstimulation = tachysystole + oxytocin + FHR abnormality.

s6.q: A tetanic uterine contraction is defined as:
options: A single contraction sustained for >3 minutes
s6.explain: A tetanic uterine contraction is a single contraction sustained for more than 3 minutes.

s6.q: Which of the following best defines this condition?
options: A pathological deviation from normal anatomy or function
An incidental finding with no clinical significance
s6.explain: A definition describes the essential nature of a condition as a deviation from normal.

s7.q: The first step in managing uterine overactivity is:
options: Positioning the patient into lateral position
s7.explain: The 5-step sequence: 1) Lateral positioning, 2) Oxygenation, 3) Discontinue oxytocin, 4) Administer tocolytic, 5) Consider immediate delivery if persistently ominous FHR tracing.

s8.q: In coordinate (normal polarity) abnormal uterine action, the fundal dominance is:
s8.explain: In coordinate abnormal uterine action, the fundal dominance is preserved — the contraction wave still starts at the fundus. Examples: precipitate labor, Bandl's ring, hypotonic inertia.

s8.q: Which of the following is an incoordinate (abnormal polarity) uterine action?
s8.explain: Constriction ring is an incoordinate uterine action where fundal dominance is lost. Others (precipitate labor, Bandl's, hypotonic inertia) are coordinate types.

s9.q: Precipitate labor is defined as labor lasting less than:
s9.explain: Precipitate labor lasts less than 3 hours. It is more common in multiparas with strong contractions, small baby, and roomy pelvis.

s9.q: Which is a fetal complication of precipitate labor?
options: Intracranial hemorrhage due to sudden compression/decompression of head
s9.explain: Fetal complications include intracranial hemorrhage (from sudden compression/decompression), fetal asphyxia, cord avulsion, and fetal injury. Maternal complications include lacerations, abruption, shock, inversion, PPH, and sepsis.

s10.q: Bandl's (pathological retraction) ring is a sign of:
s10.explain: Bandl's ring is a sign of obstructed labor with impending uterine rupture. The upper segment retracts and thickens while the lower segment is markedly stretched and thinned.

s10.q: Pathological retraction ring can be detected by:
options: Visible abdominally and palpable as a transverse groove
s10.explain: Bandl's ring is visible abdominally and palpable as a transverse groove rising between symphysis and umbilicus — in contrast to constriction ring which is felt only vaginally.

s12.q: Hypotonic uterine inertia is characterized by:
options: Irregular painful contractions with no relaxation
s12.explain: Hypotonic inertia features infrequent weak uterine contractions with slow cervical dilatation. Management includes amniotomy and oxytocin augmentation after excluding CPD.

s12.q: Which is a contraindication to oxytocin augmentation?
s12.explain: Contraindications to oxytocin include cephalopelvic disproportion, malpresentation, scarred uterus, and grand multipara.

s13.q: A key difference between pathological retraction ring and constriction ring is:
options: Constriction ring is felt only vaginally; Bandl's ring is seen and felt abdominally
Constriction ring rises upward; Bandl's ring is fixed
Bandl's ring is localized; constriction ring is global
Both are managed the same way
s13.explain: Bandl's ring is palpable abdominally as a rising transverse groove. Constriction ring is felt only vaginally and does not change position. This is the key bedside discriminator.
